jurisdiction: Council of Europe / European Court of Human Rights (Strasbourg)

DOCTRINE_MARGIN_OF_APPRECIATION.label: Margin of Appreciation
DOCTRINE_MARGIN_OF_APPRECIATION.establishing_cases: Handyside v. UK (1976), Sunday Times v. UK (1979), Rasmussen v. Denmark (1984), Evans v. UK [GC] (2007), Hirst v. UK (No. 2) [GC] (2005), Animal Defenders International v. UK [GC] (2013)
requires: [AND — width determined by three factors]
Nature of the right involved
Legitimate aim pursued by the restriction
Presence or absence of European consensus among Contracting States
alternatives: Intimate aspects of private life at stake (Dudgeon v. UK, 1981)
European consensus exists (Sunday Times v. UK, 1979)
Suspect grounds of discrimination (D.H. v. Czech Republic [GC], 2007)
defeaters: Margin is never unlimited — European supervision always accompanies it
Court retains final authority on necessity in a democratic society
Wide margin applies where: morals (Handyside), national security (Klass), no consensus (S.H. v. Austria [GC], 2011), democratic deliberation (Animal Defenders International)
DOCTRINE_MARGIN_OF_APPRECIATION.typical_edge_pattern: (State_Interference) --[assessed via]--> (Margin_of_Appreciation) --[width determined by]--> (Consensus + Right_Nature + Aim) --[if exceeded]--> (Violation)
DOCTRINE_MARGIN_OF_APPRECIATION.key_phrases: "margin of appreciation", "vital forces of their countries", "European supervision", "common ground among Contracting States", "wide margin", "narrow margin"

DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_NECESSARY_IN_DE.label: Proportionality / Necessary in a Democratic Society
DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_NECESSARY_IN_DE.establishing_cases: Handyside v. UK (1976), Sunday Times v. UK (1979), Dudgeon v. UK (1981), Silver v. UK (1983), Olsson v. Sweden (No. 1) (1988)
requires: [AND — three-part test]
Prescribed by law (accessible and foreseeable legal basis)
Legitimate aim (from exhaustive list in relevant article)
Necessary in a democratic society (pressing social need + proportionate + relevant and sufficient reasons)
defeaters: No legal basis in domestic law
Aim not within exhaustive list
No pressing social need demonstrated
Reasons not relevant and sufficient
Disproportionate to aim pursued
'Necessary' does not mean useful, reasonable, or desirable (Handyside §48)
DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_NECESSARY_IN_DE.key_phrases: "pressing social need", "relevant and sufficient reasons", "proportionate to the legitimate aim", "necessary in a democratic society", "fair balance", "narrowly interpreted"

DOCTRINE_LIVING_INSTRUMENT.label: Living Instrument Doctrine
DOCTRINE_LIVING_INSTRUMENT.establishing_cases: Tyrer v. UK (1978), Marckx v. Belgium (1979), Christine Goodwin v. UK [GC] (2002), Demir and Baykara v. Turkey [GC] (2008)
requires: [OR — sources of evolving standards]
European consensus (survey of Contracting States' laws)
International instruments (treaties, resolutions, Council of Europe, UN, ILO)
Emerging trends or scientific developments
Comparative law analysis
defeaters: No European consensus (margin remains wider)
Consensus is indicative, not dispositive
DOCTRINE_LIVING_INSTRUMENT.key_phrases: "living instrument", "present-day conditions", "practical and effective", "theoretical or illusory", "increasingly high standard"

DOCTRINE_AUTONOMOUS_CONCEPTS.label: Autonomous Concepts
DOCTRINE_AUTONOMOUS_CONCEPTS.establishing_cases: Engel v. Netherlands (1976) — see also DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA, Ringeisen v. Austria (1971), König v. Germany (1978), Öztürk v. Germany (1984), Chassagnou v. France (1999), Niemietz v. Germany (1992)
requires: [AND]
Convention term at issue
Domestic classification is not decisive but no more than a starting point
Court examines substantive content and effects
alternatives: [OR — Engel criteria for 'criminal charge' are alternative, not cumulative]
Classification under national law (starting point only)
defeaters: Convention term has no independent European meaning (rare)
Domestic classification may be relevant as starting point
DOCTRINE_AUTONOMOUS_CONCEPTS.key_phrases: "autonomous concept", "substantive content and effects", "not decisive", "no more than a starting point", "Engel criteria", "civil rights and obligations", "criminal charge"

DOCTRINE_PRESCRIBED_BY_LAW.label: Prescribed by Law Test
DOCTRINE_PRESCRIBED_BY_LAW.establishing_cases: Sunday Times v. UK (1979), Malone v. UK (1984), Kruslin v. France (1990), Hasan and Chaush v. Bulgaria [GC] (2000)
requires: [AND — three requirements]
Legal basis in domestic law (substantive, not formal sense)
Accessibility (citizen can ascertain applicable rules)
Foreseeability (sufficient precision to regulate conduct)
defeaters: No legal basis in domestic law
Law not accessible
Insufficiently precise for foreseeability
Unfettered executive discretion (Hasan and Chaush)
Secret power without safeguards (Malone)
DOCTRINE_PRESCRIBED_BY_LAW.key_phrases: "prescribed by law", "in accordance with the law", "accessible and foreseeable", "quality of the law", "rule of law", "unfettered power"

DOCTRINE_POSITIVE_OBLIGATIONS.label: Positive Obligations Doctrine
DOCTRINE_POSITIVE_OBLIGATIONS.parent: Convention rights effectiveness (Airey v. Ireland principle)
DOCTRINE_POSITIVE_OBLIGATIONS.establishing_cases: Marckx v. Belgium (1979), Airey v. Ireland (1979), X and Y v. Netherlands (1985), Platform Ärzte für das Leben v. Austria (1988)
requires: [AND]
Convention right engaged
State must take active measures to secure rights effectively
Fair balance between individual and community interests
Rights must be practical and effective, not theoretical and illusory
Obligation must not impose impossible or disproportionate burden (Osman §116)
defeaters: Would impose impossible or disproportionate burden
Fair balance favours community interest
State has margin of appreciation in choosing means
DOCTRINE_POSITIVE_OBLIGATIONS.key_phrases: "positive obligations inherent in", "practical and effective", "theoretical and illusory", "fair balance", "impossible or disproportionate burden"

DOCTRINE_OSMAN_TEST_ARTICLE_2.label: Osman Test (Article 2 Operational Duty)
DOCTRINE_OSMAN_TEST_ARTICLE_2.establishing_cases: Osman v. UK (1998), Keenan v. UK (2001), Öneryıldız v. Turkey [GC] (2004), Kurt v. Austria [GC] (2021)
requires: [AND — five elements]
Authorities knew or ought to have known at the time
Of a real and immediate risk to the life
Of an identified individual or individuals
Arising from criminal acts of another
Failed to take measures within the scope of their powers which might have been expected to avoid that risk
defeaters: Not every claimed risk entails operational measures
Difficulties in policing modern societies
Unpredictability of human conduct
Operational choices in priorities and resources
DOCTRINE_OSMAN_TEST_ARTICLE_2.key_phrases: "knew or ought to have known", "real and immediate risk to life", "identified individual", "within the scope of their powers", "risk assessment obligation"

DOCTRINE_ABSOLUTE_RIGHTS.label: Absolute Rights (Articles 3, 4§1, 7)
DOCTRINE_ABSOLUTE_RIGHTS.establishing_cases: Chahal v. UK (1996), Saadi v. Italy [GC] (2008), Gäfgen v. Germany [GC] (2010)
requires: [AND — three elements of absoluteness]
No qualifying paragraph permitting interference
Article 15§2 prohibits derogation
Protection unconditional irrespective of conduct of person concerned
defeaters: None — absolute rights admit no exceptions, no derogation, no balancing
DOCTRINE_ABSOLUTE_RIGHTS.key_phrases: "absolute", "no derogation", "irrespective of the conduct", "cannot be a material consideration", "non-refoulement", "real risk"

DOCTRINE_ESSENCE_OF_THE_RIGHT.label: Essence of the Right / Core Right Protection
DOCTRINE_ESSENCE_OF_THE_RIGHT.establishing_cases: Ashingdane v. UK (1985), Belgian Linguistic Case (1968), Mathieu-Mohin v. Belgium (1987)
requires: [AND — Ashingdane criteria]
Limitation must not restrict access to extent that essence is impaired
Must pursue a legitimate aim
Reasonable proportionality between means and aim
defeaters: Essence not impaired
Legitimate aim established
Proportionate means
DOCTRINE_ESSENCE_OF_THE_RIGHT.key_phrases: "very essence of the right", "injure the substance", "Ashingdane criteria", "impair their very essence"

DOCTRINE_NON_DISCRIMINATION_ARTICLE_14.label: Non-Discrimination (Article 14)
DOCTRINE_NON_DISCRIMINATION_ARTICLE_14.establishing_cases: Belgian Linguistic Case (1968), Abdulaziz v. UK (1985), Thlimmenos v. Greece [GC] (2000), D.H. v. Czech Republic [GC] (2007), Burden v. UK [GC] (2008)
requires: [AND — four elements]
Ambit — facts within another Convention right
Differential treatment of persons in relevantly similar situations
Treatment on a prohibited ground (illustrative list including 'other status')
No objective and reasonable justification (legitimate aim + proportionate means)
alternatives: Treating similar situations differently without justification
Failing to treat significantly different situations differently without justification (Thlimmenos)
defeaters: Facts not within ambit of another Convention right
Persons not in relevantly similar situations
Objective and reasonable justification exists
Proportionate means to legitimate aim
DOCTRINE_NON_DISCRIMINATION_ARTICLE_14.key_phrases: "relevantly similar situations", "very weighty reasons", "objective and reasonable justification", "indirect discrimination", "Thlimmenos principle", "disproportionately prejudicial effects"

DOCTRINE_SUBSTANTIVE_VS_PROCEDURAL_OBLIG.label: Substantive vs. Procedural Obligations (Articles 2 and 3)
DOCTRINE_SUBSTANTIVE_VS_PROCEDURAL_OBLIG.establishing_cases: McCann v. UK (1995), Jordan v. UK (2001), Assenov v. Bulgaria (1998), Šilih v. Slovenia [GC] (2009)
requires: [AND — Jordan five requirements for effective investigation]
Independence of investigators
Adequacy — capable of identifying and punishing those responsible
Promptness and reasonable expedition
Public scrutiny
Involvement of next-of-kin
defeaters: Investigation met all five requirements
Genuine attempt despite obstacles
DOCTRINE_SUBSTANTIVE_VS_PROCEDURAL_OBLIG.key_phrases: "separate and autonomous duty", "effective investigation", "of its own motion", "procedural obligation", "independence of investigators"

DOCTRINE_EXHAUSTION_DOMESTIC_REMEDIES.label: Exhaustion of Domestic Remedies (Article 35§1)
DOCTRINE_EXHAUSTION_DOMESTIC_REMEDIES.establishing_cases: Akdivar v. Turkey [GC] (1996), Vučković v. Serbia [GC] (2014)
requires: [AND]
Remedies must be available (sufficiently certain in practice)
Remedies must be effective (capable of providing redress)
Remedies must be sufficient
Applied with flexibility and without excessive formalism (Akdivar §69)
alternatives: Special circumstances (climate of fear, official obstruction)
Administrative practice of repeated violations with official tolerance
defeaters: Government shows remedy was available and effective
Applicant fails to show exhaustion or special circumstances
DOCTRINE_EXHAUSTION_DOMESTIC_REMEDIES.key_phrases: "exhaust domestic remedies", "flexibility and without excessive formalism", "available and effective", "administrative practice", "official tolerance"

DOCTRINE_TIME_LIMIT_RULE.label: Four-Month Rule (formerly Six-Month)
DOCTRINE_TIME_LIMIT_RULE.establishing_cases: Protocol No. 15 (effective 1 February 2022), Varnava v. Turkey [GC] (2009)
requires: [AND]
Application within four months of final domestic decision
Where no remedy available: time runs from act or awareness
Non-retroactive: applies only where final decision from 1 Feb 2022 onwards
alternatives: Ongoing state of affairs: time-limit starts afresh each day (Varnava §159)
Enforced disappearances, ongoing property deprivation, continuing legislative situations
defeaters: Time limit expired
No continuing situation established
DOCTRINE_TIME_LIMIT_RULE.key_phrases: "four-month rule", "final domestic decision", "continuing situation", "starts afresh each day"

DOCTRINE_FOURTH_INSTANCE.label: Fourth Instance Doctrine / Duty to Give Reasons
DOCTRINE_FOURTH_INSTANCE.establishing_cases: García Ruiz v. Spain [GC] (1999), Ruiz Torija v. Spain (1994), Hiro Balani v. Spain (1994), Taxquet v. Belgium [GC] (2010), Moreira Ferreira v. Portugal (No. 2) [GC] (2017)
requires: [AND — two interlinked principles]
Fourth instance bar: Court will not deal with errors of fact or law unless they infringe Convention rights (García Ruiz §26)
Duty to give reasons: courts must adequately state reasons on which decisions are based
Extent of duty varies according to the nature of the decision
Courts must address submissions that, if accepted, would be decisive for the outcome (Ruiz Torija)
No requirement for a detailed answer to every argument
alternatives: Appellate court may simply endorse lower court's reasons
Jury verdicts: unreasoned general verdicts compatible with Art 6 if adequate safeguards exist (Taxquet [GC])
Precise questions + clear judicial directions + available appeal = sufficient for jury trials
defeaters: Findings not arbitrary or manifestly unreasonable
No Convention right infringed
Court addressed all decisive arguments
Reasoning adequate for the type and level of decision
Conclusions not grossly arbitrary or amounting to denial of justice
DOCTRINE_FOURTH_INSTANCE.key_phrases: "fourth instance", "arbitrary or manifestly unreasonable", "errors of fact or law", "sufficiently reasoned", "adequately reasoned", "obligation to give reasons", "duty to give reasons", "reasoned judgment", "detailed answer to every argument", "decisive for the outcome", "proper administration of justice", "nature of the decision"

DOCTRINE_PILOT_JUDGMENT.label: Pilot Judgment Procedure
DOCTRINE_PILOT_JUDGMENT.parent: Rule 61 of the Rules of Court
DOCTRINE_PILOT_JUDGMENT.establishing_cases: Broniowski v. Poland [GC] (2004), Hutten-Czapska v. Poland [GC] (2006), Burdov v. Russia (No. 2) (2009)
requires: [AND]
Systemic or structural problem generating similar applications
Court identifies the problem
Court specifies general measures required
May set time limits and freeze similar pending cases
defeaters: No systemic problem established
Individual case only
DOCTRINE_PILOT_JUDGMENT.key_phrases: "systemic or structural problem", "pilot judgment", "general measures", "freeze similar cases"

DOCTRINE_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY_ARTICLE_13.label: Effective Remedy (Article 13)
DOCTRINE_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY_ARTICLE_13.establishing_cases: Boyle and Rice v. UK (1988), Silver v. UK (1983), Kudła v. Poland [GC] (2000)
requires: [AND]
Arguable complaint under another Convention provision
Remedy allows competent authority to deal with substance
Remedy grants appropriate relief
Aggregate of remedies may suffice (Silver; Kudła)
defeaters: No arguable complaint
Aggregate of remedies provides effective relief

DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_AIM.label: Legitimate Aim Analysis
DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_AIM.establishing_cases: Handyside v. UK (1976), Sunday Times v. UK (1979)
requires: [AND]
Government identifies aim
Aim falls within exhaustive list of relevant article
Typically not contentious — most cases turn on necessity
defeaters: Aim not within exhaustive list
Pretext — real aim differs from stated aim
DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_AIM.key_phrases: "legitimate aim", "exhaustive list", "national security", "public safety", "economic well-being", "prevention of disorder or crime", "protection of health or morals", "rights and freedoms of others"

DOCTRINE_DEROGATION_ARTICLE_15.label: Derogation Under Article 15
DOCTRINE_DEROGATION_ARTICLE_15.establishing_cases: Lawless v. Ireland (No. 3) (1961), Ireland v. UK (1978), A. and Others v. UK [GC] (2009)
requires: [AND — three conditions]
War or other public emergency threatening the life of the nation
Measures strictly required by the exigencies of the situation
Consistency with other international law obligations
defeaters: Non-derogable rights: Articles 2 (except lawful acts of war), 3, 4§1, 7, P6-Art3, P7-Art4, P13-Art2
Measures disproportionate (A. v. UK [GC], 2009)
Failure to notify Secretary General (Art. 15§3)
DOCTRINE_DEROGATION_ARTICLE_15.key_phrases: "public emergency threatening the life of the nation", "strictly required by the exigencies", "non-derogable", "exceptional situation of crisis"

DOCTRINE_LOSS_OF_VICTIM_STATUS.label: Loss of Victim Status
DOCTRINE_LOSS_OF_VICTIM_STATUS.establishing_cases: Eckle v. Germany (1982), Scordino v. Italy (No. 1) [GC] (2006)
requires: [AND — both must be met]
National authorities acknowledged the breach (expressly or in substance)
Afforded adequate and appropriate redress
defeaters: Only acknowledgment without redress — status retained
Only redress without acknowledgment — status retained
DOCTRINE_LOSS_OF_VICTIM_STATUS.key_phrases: "acknowledged the breach", "adequate and appropriate redress", "victim status", "directly affected"

DOCTRINE_CONTINUING_SITUATION.label: Continuing Situation Doctrine
DOCTRINE_CONTINUING_SITUATION.establishing_cases: Varnava v. Turkey [GC] (2009), Papamichalopoulos v. Greece (1993)
requires: [AND]
Ongoing state of affairs constituting a breach
Each day the situation continues constitutes renewed breach
defeaters: Situation has ceased
Single instantaneous act, not continuing
DOCTRINE_CONTINUING_SITUATION.key_phrases: "continuing situation", "starts afresh each day", "ongoing state of affairs", "enforced disappearance"

ARTICLE_2_RIGHT_TO_LIFE.label: Article 2 — Right to Life
ARTICLE_2_RIGHT_TO_LIFE.establishing_cases: McCann v. UK (1995), Osman v. UK (1998), Öneryıldız v. Turkey [GC] (2004), Budayeva v. Russia (2008), Kurt v. Austria [GC] (2021)
requires: [AND — stricter than necessity test]
Force must be no more than absolutely necessary
Three obligation types: substantive negative (refrain from killing), substantive positive (regulatory framework + Osman duty), procedural (effective investigation)
alternatives: [OR — Art 2§2 permitted exceptions to lethal force]
defeaters: Force was absolutely necessary under Art 2§2
Effective investigation was conducted
ARTICLE_2_RIGHT_TO_LIFE.key_phrases: "no more than absolutely necessary", "right to life", "effective investigation", "regulatory framework", "operational duty"

ARTICLE_3_PROHIBITION_OF_TORTURE.label: Article 3 — Prohibition of Torture
ARTICLE_3_PROHIBITION_OF_TORTURE.establishing_cases: Ireland v. UK (1978), Soering v. UK (1989), Chahal v. UK (1996), Selmouni v. France [GC] (1999), Saadi v. Italy [GC] (2008), M.S.S. v. Belgium and Greece [GC] (2011); for medical expulsion threshold see DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION
requires: [AND]
Minimum level of severity threshold (relative: duration, effects, victim characteristics)
Classification: torture (deliberate, purposive) > inhuman treatment (intense suffering) > degrading treatment (humiliation)
defeaters: None — absolute, no exceptions, no derogation, no balancing
ARTICLE_3_PROHIBITION_OF_TORTURE.key_phrases: "minimum level of severity", "absolute prohibition", "no derogation", "non-refoulement", "substantial grounds for believing", "real risk", "most fundamental values"

ARTICLE_4_PROHIBITION_SLAVERY.label: Article 4 — Prohibition of Slavery and Forced Labour
ARTICLE_4_PROHIBITION_SLAVERY.establishing_cases: Siliadin v. France (2005), Rantsev v. Cyprus and Russia (2010), Chowdury v. Greece (2017), S.M. v. Croatia [GC] (2020)
requires: [OR — different thresholds]
Slavery: exercise of powers of ownership over a person (Siliadin)
Servitude: obligation to provide services under coercion, with no prospect of change
Forced labour: menace of penalty + involuntariness (ILO Convention No. 29)
Trafficking: recruitment/transport/transfer by threat/coercion for exploitation (Rantsev)
defeaters: Activity falls within Art 4§3 exclusions
Voluntary consent (genuinely free)
ARTICLE_4_PROHIBITION_SLAVERY.key_phrases: "powers of ownership", "menace of penalty", "involuntariness", "modern form of slave trade", "human trafficking", "forced labour"

ARTICLE_5_RIGHT_TO_LIBERTY.label: Article 5 — Right to Liberty and Security
ARTICLE_5_RIGHT_TO_LIBERTY.establishing_cases: Winterwerp v. Netherlands (1979), Brogan v. UK (1988), Saadi v. UK [GC] (2008)
requires: [AND — lawfulness test]
Detention falls within one of Art 5§1(a)–(f) exhaustive grounds
In accordance with a procedure prescribed by law
Lawful under domestic and Convention law
Not arbitrary
alternatives: (e) Unsound mind, alcoholics, vagrants, infectious diseases
defeaters: Detention falls within permitted ground
Lawful and not arbitrary
Procedural safeguards met (§§2-5: reasons, prompt judge, habeas corpus, compensation)
ARTICLE_5_RIGHT_TO_LIBERTY.key_phrases: "exhaustive list", "narrow interpretation", "prescribed by law", "reasonable suspicion", "promptly brought before judge", "habeas corpus", "arbitrary"

ARTICLE_6_RIGHT_TO_FAIR_TRIAL.label: Article 6 — Right to a Fair Trial
ARTICLE_6_RIGHT_TO_FAIR_TRIAL.establishing_cases: Golder v. UK (1975), König v. Germany (1978), Engel v. Netherlands (1976), Salduz v. Turkey [GC] (2008), Ibrahim v. UK [GC] (2016), Beuze v. Belgium [GC] (2018)
requires: [AND — core guarantees §1]
Fair and public hearing
Independent and impartial tribunal established by law
Within reasonable time
Civil limb: 'civil rights and obligations' (autonomous — König)
Criminal limb: 'criminal charge' (Engel criteria)
alternatives: Adequate time and facilities for defence
defeaters: Fair trial overall despite individual imperfections
Limitation does not impair essence of right (Ashingdane)
ARTICLE_6_RIGHT_TO_FAIR_TRIAL.key_phrases: "fair trial", "independent and impartial tribunal", "reasonable time", "access to court", "presumption of innocence", "right to silence", "equality of arms", "no justification for interpreting restrictively"

ARTICLE_7_NULLUM_CRIMEN.label: Article 7 — No Punishment Without Law
ARTICLE_7_NULLUM_CRIMEN.establishing_cases: Kokkinakis v. Greece (1993), S.W. v. UK (1995), Scoppola v. Italy (No. 2) [GC] (2009), Del Río Prada v. Spain [GC] (2013)
requires: [AND — four principles]
Only the law can define a crime (nullum crimen sine lege)
Only the law can prescribe a penalty (nulla poena sine lege)
No extensive construction by analogy to accused's disadvantage
Non-retroactivity of heavier criminal penalties
alternatives: Judicial development following a perceptible line of case law (S.W. v. UK)
Retrospective application of more favourable law guaranteed (Scoppola [GC])
defeaters: Law was accessible and foreseeable at time of conduct
Development followed perceptible case-law line
ARTICLE_7_NULLUM_CRIMEN.key_phrases: "nullum crimen sine lege", "nulla poena sine lege", "accessible and foreseeable", "perceptible line of case law", "lex mitior", "non-retroactivity"

ARTICLE_8_PRIVATE_FAMILY_LIFE.label: Article 8 — Private and Family Life
ARTICLE_8_PRIVATE_FAMILY_LIFE.establishing_cases: Dudgeon v. UK (1981), López Ostra v. Spain (1994), Von Hannover v. Germany (No. 2) [GC] (2012), S. and Marper v. UK (2008), Zakharov v. Russia (2015); for Art 8 vs Art 10 balancing see DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING
requires: [AND — four-step interference test]
Interference with private life, family life, home, or correspondence
Prescribed by law (accessible + foreseeable)
Legitimate aim from Art 8§2 list
Necessary in a democratic society
defeaters: Interference prescribed by law
Pursued legitimate aim
Necessary and proportionate
State acted within margin of appreciation
ARTICLE_8_PRIVATE_FAMILY_LIFE.key_phrases: "private life", "family life", "home", "correspondence", "personal data", "physical and psychological integrity", "environmental pollution", "surveillance safeguards"

ARTICLE_9_FREEDOM_RELIGION.label: Article 9 — Freedom of Thought, Conscience and Religion
ARTICLE_9_FREEDOM_RELIGION.parent: Forum internum absolute; forum externum qualified
ARTICLE_9_FREEDOM_RELIGION.establishing_cases: Kokkinakis v. Greece (1993), Leyla Şahin v. Turkey [GC] (2005), Lautsi v. Italy [GC] (2011), S.A.S. v. France (2014), Eweida v. UK (2013)
requires: [AND — for manifestation restriction]
Manifestation intimately linked to belief (Eweida)
Belief shows cogency, seriousness, cohesion and importance
If restricted: prescribed by law + legitimate aim + necessary
defeaters: Forum internum: never justifiable to restrict
Forum externum: restriction meets three-part test
Wide margin for secularism arrangements (Şahin)
ARTICLE_9_FREEDOM_RELIGION.key_phrases: "forum internum", "forum externum", "foundations of democratic society", "cogency seriousness cohesion importance", "intimately linked", "living together"

ARTICLE_10_FREEDOM_OF_EXPRESSION.label: Article 10 — Freedom of Expression
ARTICLE_10_FREEDOM_OF_EXPRESSION.establishing_cases: Lingens v. Austria (1986), Jersild v. Denmark (1994), Goodwin v. UK (1996), Delfi AS v. Estonia [GC] (2015)
requires: [AND — interference test]
Interference with expression (information, ideas — including those that offend, shock or disturb)
Prescribed by law
Legitimate aim from Art 10§2 list
Necessary in a democratic society
defeaters: Proportionate restriction
Duties and responsibilities (Art 10§2)
Facts vs value judgments distinction (Lingens)
Politicians must tolerate greater criticism
ARTICLE_10_FREEDOM_OF_EXPRESSION.key_phrases: "essential foundations of democratic society", "chilling effect", "public watchdog", "offend shock or disturb", "value judgments", "source protection", "duties and responsibilities"

ARTICLE_11_FREEDOM_ASSEMBLY_ASSOCIATION.label: Article 11 — Freedom of Assembly and Association
ARTICLE_11_FREEDOM_ASSEMBLY_ASSOCIATION.establishing_cases: United Communist Party v. Turkey (1998), Refah Partisi v. Turkey [GC] (2003), Demir and Baykara v. Turkey [GC] (2008), Kudrevičius v. Lithuania [GC] (2015)
requires: [AND — interference test]
Interference with peaceful assembly or association (including trade unions, political parties)
Prescribed by law
Legitimate aim from Art 11§2 list
Necessary in a democratic society
defeaters: Assembly not peaceful
Proportionate restriction meeting three-part test
Political party dissolution: convincing evidence of concrete threat to democracy (Refah Partisi)
ARTICLE_11_FREEDOM_ASSEMBLY_ASSOCIATION.key_phrases: "peaceful assembly", "freedom of association", "political parties essential to democracy", "degree of tolerance", "collective bargaining", "trade union", "dissolution"

ARTICLE_13_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY.label: Article 13 — Effective Remedy
ARTICLE_13_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY.parent: Requires arguable complaint under another provision
ARTICLE_13_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY.establishing_cases: Boyle and Rice v. UK (1988), Silver v. UK (1983), Kudła v. Poland [GC] (2000)
requires: [AND]
Arguable complaint under another Convention provision
National authority competent to deal with substance
Remedy grants appropriate relief
Aggregate of remedies may suffice
defeaters: No arguable complaint
Aggregate provides effective relief
ARTICLE_13_EFFECTIVE_REMEDY.key_phrases: "arguable complaint", "effective remedy", "appropriate relief", "aggregate of remedies", "competent national authority"

ARTICLE_14_PROHIBITION_DISCRIMINATION.label: Article 14 — Prohibition of Discrimination
ARTICLE_14_PROHIBITION_DISCRIMINATION.parent: Accessory — must be invoked with another Convention right
ARTICLE_14_PROHIBITION_DISCRIMINATION.establishing_cases: Belgian Linguistic Case (1968), Abdulaziz v. UK (1985), Thlimmenos v. Greece [GC] (2000), D.H. v. Czech Republic [GC] (2007)
requires: [AND — four elements]
Facts within ambit of another Convention right
Differential treatment of persons in relevantly similar situations
Treatment on prohibited ground (illustrative list including other status)
No objective and reasonable justification
alternatives: Failing to treat different differently without justification
defeaters: Not within ambit
Not relevantly similar
Objective and reasonable justification (very weighty reasons for suspect grounds)
ARTICLE_14_PROHIBITION_DISCRIMINATION.key_phrases: "within the ambit", "relevantly similar situations", "very weighty reasons", "objective and reasonable justification", "other status", "indirect discrimination"

ARTICLES_34_35_ADMISSIBILITY.label: Articles 34–35 — Standing and Admissibility
ARTICLES_34_35_ADMISSIBILITY.establishing_cases: Akdivar v. Turkey [GC] (1996), Vučković v. Serbia [GC] (2014)
requires: [AND — Art 34 + Art 35 combined]
Victim: applicant directly affected (autonomous concept)
Exhaustion of domestic remedies
Four-month time limit
Not manifestly ill-founded
No significant disadvantage (de minimis)
Not same as previous application
Not anonymous
Compatible ratione materiae/temporis/personae/loci
No abuse of right of application
defeaters: Any admissibility criterion not met
ARTICLES_34_35_ADMISSIBILITY.key_phrases: "victim", "directly affected", "manifestly ill-founded", "significant disadvantage", "ratione materiae", "ratione temporis", "ratione personae", "abuse of right"

PROTOCOL_1_ART_1_PROPERTY.label: Protocol 1, Article 1 — Protection of Property
PROTOCOL_1_ART_1_PROPERTY.parent: Three rules from Sporrong and Lönnroth (1982)
PROTOCOL_1_ART_1_PROPERTY.establishing_cases: Sporrong and Lönnroth v. Sweden (1982), James v. UK (1986), Beyeler v. Italy [GC] (2000)
requires: [AND — Sporrong three rules]
Rule 1 (general): peaceful enjoyment of possessions
Rule 2 (deprivation): no deprivation except in public interest and subject to law
Rule 3 (control of use): state may control use in general interest or for taxes
Rules 2 and 3 are particular instances of Rule 1
defeaters: Deprivation in public interest with lawful basis
Fair balance struck
Possessions include: economic interests, claims, legitimate expectations (Beyeler)
PROTOCOL_1_ART_1_PROPERTY.key_phrases: "peaceful enjoyment of possessions", "public interest", "fair balance", "legitimate expectations", "control of use", "deprivation"

PROTOCOL_1_ART_2_EDUCATION.label: Protocol 1, Article 2 — Right to Education
PROTOCOL_1_ART_2_EDUCATION.parent: Negative formulation (no person shall be denied)
PROTOCOL_1_ART_2_EDUCATION.establishing_cases: Belgian Linguistic Case (1968), Campbell and Cosans v. UK (1982)
requires: [AND]
Access to existing educational institutions
Official recognition of studies completed
Parents' religious/philosophical convictions respected (second sentence)
Regulation must not injure substance of right
defeaters: Regulation does not injure substance
State has wide latitude in organizing education
PROTOCOL_1_ART_2_EDUCATION.key_phrases: "no person shall be denied", "substance of the right", "religious and philosophical convictions", "calls for regulation by the State"

PROTOCOL_1_ART_3_FREE_ELECTIONS.label: Protocol 1, Article 3 — Right to Free Elections
PROTOCOL_1_ART_3_FREE_ELECTIONS.parent: Institutional guarantee with implied individual rights
PROTOCOL_1_ART_3_FREE_ELECTIONS.establishing_cases: Mathieu-Mohin v. Belgium (1987), Hirst v. UK (No. 2) [GC] (2005)
requires: [AND]
Right to vote and to stand for election (Mathieu-Mohin)
Subject to implied limitations
Limitations must not impair very essence of right
Must serve legitimate aim with proportionate means
defeaters: Limitation does not impair essence
Legitimate aim with proportionate means
PROTOCOL_1_ART_3_FREE_ELECTIONS.key_phrases: "free elections", "right to vote", "right to stand", "very essence", "general automatic and indiscriminate restriction", "outside any acceptable margin"

PROTOCOL_7_ART_4_NE_BIS_IN_IDEM.label: Protocol 7, Article 4 — Ne Bis In Idem
PROTOCOL_7_ART_4_NE_BIS_IN_IDEM.establishing_cases: Sergey Zolotukhin v. Russia [GC] (2009), A and B v. Norway [GC] (2016)
requires: [AND — idem factum approach; 'criminal' determined by Engel criteria (see DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA)]
Offences arise from identical facts or facts substantially the same
Not based on legal classification
Dual-track exception: sufficiently close connection in substance and time forming integrated whole (A and B v. Norway)
defeaters: Offences arise from different facts
Dual proceedings have sufficiently close connection (A and B v. Norway)
PROTOCOL_7_ART_4_NE_BIS_IN_IDEM.key_phrases: "idem factum", "identical facts", "substantially the same", "sufficiently close connection in substance and time", "ne bis in idem"

PROTOCOL_12_ART_1_GENERAL_DISCRIMINATION.label: Protocol 12, Article 1 — General Prohibition of Discrimination
PROTOCOL_12_ART_1_GENERAL_DISCRIMINATION.establishing_cases: Sejdić and Finci v. Bosnia and Herzegovina [GC] (2009)
requires: [AND]
Discrimination in enjoyment of any right set forth by law (including domestic law)
Same substantive test as Art 14 (objective and reasonable justification)
Broader scope: not limited to Convention rights
defeaters: Objective and reasonable justification
Proportionate means to legitimate aim
PROTOCOL_12_ART_1_GENERAL_DISCRIMINATION.key_phrases: "any right set forth by law", "free-standing", "broader scope than Article 14", "objective and reasonable justification"

ARTICLE_1_JURISDICTION.label: Article 1 — Jurisdiction (Threshold Criterion)
ARTICLE_1_JURISDICTION.establishing_cases: Banković v. Belgium [GC] (2001, dec.), Loizidou v. Turkey (1995), Al-Skeini v. UK [GC] (2011), Catan v. Moldova and Russia [GC] (2012), Hassan v. UK [GC] (2014), Jaloud v. Netherlands [GC] (2014), M.N. v. Belgium [GC] (2020), Georgia v. Russia (II) [GC] (2021), Hanan v. Germany [GC] (2021)
requires: [OR — three models of extraterritorial jurisdiction (plus territorial default)]
Territorial jurisdiction: primarily territorial, default model

Model 1 — Effective control of territory (spatial): State exercises effective overall control of an area outside national territory through military forces or subordinate local administration (Loizidou, Catan)

Model 2 — State agent authority and control (personal): State agents exercise physical power and control over an individual through use of force (Al-Skeini §§136-137); applies to custody, detention, checkpoints, interception at sea

Model 3 — Exercise of public powers (functional): State exercises all or some public powers normally exercised by the territorial government, through consent, invitation, or acquiescence (Al-Skeini §135)
alternatives: [OR — scope of obligation varies by model]
Effective control of territory: must secure entire range of substantive rights
State agent authority/control: only rights relevant to the situation of that individual
Rights may be divided and tailored (Al-Skeini §137, overruling Banković on this point)
defeaters: Primarily territorial principle: extraterritorial jurisdiction requires special justification
Active hostilities exclusion: during context of chaos of armed confrontation, neither model applies (Georgia v. Russia (II) §§137-138)
Embassy premises alone insufficient for jurisdiction (M.N. v. Belgium [GC])
No cause-and-effect jurisdiction
Attribution to international organization bars State responsibility (Behrami and Behrami v. France, 2007)
Espace juridique concept (substantially diminished post-Al-Skeini but not fully abandoned)
ARTICLE_1_JURISDICTION.key_phrases: "within their jurisdiction", "jurisdiction within the meaning of Article 1", "threshold criterion", "primarily territorial", "effective control", "effective overall control", "state agent authority", "authority and control", "physical power and control", "espace juridique", "legal space", "extraterritorial jurisdiction", "subordinate local administration", "public powers", "divided and tailored", "ratione loci"

ARTICLE_12_RIGHT_TO_MARRY.label: Article 12 — Right to Marry and Found a Family
ARTICLE_12_RIGHT_TO_MARRY.parent: Distinct from Article 8; express national law reference
ARTICLE_12_RIGHT_TO_MARRY.establishing_cases: F. v. Switzerland (1987), Goodwin v. UK [GC] (2002), Schalk and Kopf v. Austria (2010), Hämäläinen v. Finland [GC] (2014), Oliari v. Italy (2015)
requires: [AND — elements for Article 12 claim]
Men and women of marriageable age (originally biological male-female; now includes post-operative transgender persons per Goodwin [GC])
Marriageable age as determined by national law
According to national laws governing the exercise of this right — wide margin of appreciation
Permissible restrictions: capacity, consent, prohibited degrees, monogamy, procedural rules
alternatives: Article 12: formation of marital relationships only (no right to divorce: Johnston v. Ireland, 1986)
Article 8: broader sphere covering private/family life, relationships, gender identity, same-sex partnerships
Many complaints invoke both; Court frequently resolves under Article 8 alone
defeaters: Wide margin of appreciation, especially where European consensus is absent (same-sex marriage: Schalk and Kopf)
Express national law reference permits greater deference than other Convention rights
Very essence test sets the floor: states cannot impose blanket bars that entirely eliminate the right
No necessity/pressing social need test (unlike Articles 8-11)
ARTICLE_12_RIGHT_TO_MARRY.key_phrases: "right to marry", "right to marry and to found a family", "men and women of marriageable age", "national laws governing the exercise of this right", "very essence of the right to marry", "arbitrary or disproportionate", "living instrument", "European consensus", "biological criteria", "gender reassignment", "capacity to marry", "found a family"

PROTOCOL_4_ART_2_FREEDOM_OF_MOVEMENT.label: Protocol 4, Article 2 — Freedom of Movement
PROTOCOL_4_ART_2_FREEDOM_OF_MOVEMENT.parent: Three distinct rights; standard qualified-rights framework
PROTOCOL_4_ART_2_FREEDOM_OF_MOVEMENT.establishing_cases: De Tommaso v. Italy [GC] (2017), Battista v. Italy (2014), Stamose v. Bulgaria (2012), Timishev v. Russia [GC] (2005)
requires: [AND — three distinct rights, each with conditions]
Right (a): liberty of movement within the territory — requires person 'lawfully within the territory'
Right (b): freedom to choose residence — requires person 'lawfully within the territory'
Right (c): freedom to leave any country including own — applies to everyone, no lawfulness precondition
Restrictions under §3: in accordance with law + legitimate aim + necessary in a democratic society
alternatives: Protection of rights and freedoms of others
defeaters: Restriction meets quality of law standard: accessible, foreseeable, adequate safeguards (De Tommaso [GC])
Individualized assessment conducted (not blanket/automatic: Stamose)
Proportionate duration with regular review and judicial oversight
Geographically limited restrictions justified by public interest under §4
PROTOCOL_4_ART_2_FREEDOM_OF_MOVEMENT.key_phrases: "freedom of movement", "liberty of movement", "leave any country", "lawfully within the territory", "in accordance with law", "necessary in a democratic society", "ordre public", "quality of law", "foreseeability", "particular areas"

PROTOCOL_4_ART_4_COLLECTIVE_EXPULSION.label: Protocol 4, Article 4 — Prohibition of Collective Expulsion
PROTOCOL_4_ART_4_COLLECTIVE_EXPULSION.establishing_cases: N.D. and N.T. v. Spain [GC] (2020), Hirsi Jamaa v. Italy [GC] (2012), Khlaifia v. Italy [GC] (2016), Georgia v. Russia (I) [GC] (2014), Čonka v. Belgium (2002)
requires: [AND — core prohibition]
Any measure compelling aliens, as a group, to leave the country
Must be based on reasonable and objective examination of the particular case of each individual alien (Khlaifia [GC] §237)
Purpose: prevent removal without examining personal circumstances or enabling applicants to put forward arguments (Hirsi Jamaa §177)
Applies extraterritorially: interception and push-back on high seas constitutes exercise of jurisdiction (Hirsi Jamaa)
alternatives: [OR — N.D. and N.T. two-part exception test]
Condition 1: State provided genuine and effective access to means of legal entry (particularly border procedures)
Condition 2: Applicants chose not to use those means, instead crossing in unauthorized manner
If both conditions favor the State: responsibility for lack of individual assessment shifts to applicant (own culpable conduct)
defeaters: Individual assessment was conducted (even if procedurally imperfect: Khlaifia)
N.D. and N.T. exception: genuine access to legal entry existed + applicant's own culpable conduct
Individual decisions existed (but background to execution still matters: Čonka)
PROTOCOL_4_ART_4_COLLECTIVE_EXPULSION.key_phrases: "collective expulsion", "individual assessment", "reasonable and objective examination", "particular case of each individual alien", "genuine and effective access to means of legal entry", "own culpable conduct", "compelling aliens as a group to leave", "push-back", "coordinated policy"

PROTOCOL_6_13_DEATH_PENALTY_ABOLITION.label: Protocols 6 and 13 — Abolition of the Death Penalty
PROTOCOL_6_13_DEATH_PENALTY_ABOLITION.parent: Non-derogable, no reservations; P6 wartime exception; P13 absolute in all circumstances
PROTOCOL_6_13_DEATH_PENALTY_ABOLITION.establishing_cases: Soering v. UK (1989), Al-Saadoon and Mufdhi v. UK (2010), Öcalan v. Turkey [GC] (2005)
requires: [AND — absolute prohibition]
Protocol 6 Art 1: death penalty abolished; no one shall be condemned or executed (wartime exception under Art 2)
Protocol 13 Art 1: death penalty abolished in all circumstances including wartime — no exceptions
Both prohibit derogation under Article 15 and reservations under Article 57
Non-refoulement dimension: States must obtain binding assurances that death penalty will not be imposed before extraditing (Al-Saadoon)
defeaters: None for Protocol 13 — absolute in all circumstances
Protocol 6 Art 2: wartime exception only (acts prescribed by law in respect of acts committed in time of war or imminent threat of war)
Article 2 itself has been amended in effect: near-universal ratification means death penalty prohibited in all circumstances (Al-Saadoon)
PROTOCOL_6_13_DEATH_PENALTY_ABOLITION.key_phrases: "death penalty shall be abolished", "no one shall be condemned to such penalty or executed", "abolition of the death penalty", "real risk of execution", "death row phenomenon", "zone free of capital punishment", "binding assurances", "non-refoulement", "intense psychological suffering"

PROTOCOL_7_ART_2_RIGHT_OF_APPEAL.label: Protocol 7, Article 2 — Right of Appeal in Criminal Matters
PROTOCOL_7_ART_2_RIGHT_OF_APPEAL.parent: Criminal procedural guarantee; linked to Engel criteria for scope
PROTOCOL_7_ART_2_RIGHT_OF_APPEAL.establishing_cases: Gurepka v. Ukraine (2005), Galstyan v. Armenia (2007), Grosam v. Czech Republic [GC] (2023), Krombach v. France (2001)
requires: [AND — scope conditions]
Criminal offence within the meaning of Engel criteria (autonomous — confirmed Grosam [GC])
Conviction or sentence (formal finding of guilt)
By a tribunal within the meaning of Article 6
Higher tribunal must have competence to examine at minimum points of law
Leave-to-appeal systems and appeal limited to points of law are permissible (Krombach)
alternatives: Offences of a minor character as prescribed by law
Trial at first instance by the highest tribunal
Conviction following an appeal against acquittal
defeaters: Offence is minor character exception
First instance was highest tribunal
Conviction followed appeal against acquittal
Appeal right exists even if limited to points of law
PROTOCOL_7_ART_2_RIGHT_OF_APPEAL.key_phrases: "right of appeal in criminal matters", "convicted of a criminal offence", "reviewed by a higher tribunal", "conviction or sentence reviewed", "offences of a minor character", "highest tribunal", "appeal against acquittal", "full jurisdiction", "leave to appeal"

PROTOCOL_7_ART_5_EQUALITY_BETWEEN_SPOUSE.label: Protocol 7, Article 5 — Equality Between Spouses
PROTOCOL_7_ART_5_EQUALITY_BETWEEN_SPOUSE.parent: Lex specialis for spousal equality in private law; relates to Art 14 + Art 8
PROTOCOL_7_ART_5_EQUALITY_BETWEEN_SPOUSE.establishing_cases: Protocol 7, Article 5 text; standalone violations rare — typically examined under Article 14 + Article 8
requires: [AND — scope conditions]
Spouses (married couples only — not unmarried cohabitants)
Rights and responsibilities of a private law character
Covers three temporal phases: entering marriage, during marriage, upon dissolution
Covers: matrimonial property, custody, maintenance, naming rights, parental authority
alternatives: Article 5 P7: narrow scope — only private law spousal equality
Article 14 + Article 8: broader scope — all family relationships, not just spouses
Court typically examines under Art 14 + Art 8 in practice
defeaters: Child welfare exception: differential treatment permitted where necessary in the interests of the children
Not applicable to unmarried partners
Scope limited to private law character
PROTOCOL_7_ART_5_EQUALITY_BETWEEN_SPOUSE.key_phrases: "equality between spouses", "equality of rights and responsibilities", "private law character", "as to marriage, during marriage and in the event of its dissolution", "in their relations with their children", "necessary in the interests of the children"

DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA.label: Engel Criteria (Autonomous Criminal Charge Test)
DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA.parent: Article 6 criminal limb gateway; feeds into P7A2, P7A4, Article 4 P7
DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA.establishing_cases: Engel v. Netherlands (1976, Plenary), Öztürk v. Germany (1984), Jussila v. Finland [GC] (2006), A and B v. Norway [GC] (2016), Ezeh and Connors v. UK [GC] (2003)
requires: [OR/CUMULATIVE — three criteria, alternative not cumulative]
Criterion 1 — Domestic classification: only a starting point of relative weight; if classified criminal domestically, that is decisive; if disciplinary/administrative, Court looks behind the label

Criterion 2 — Nature of the offence (the more important criterion per Jussila §38): generally binding character of rule, punitive and deterrent purpose, connection to finding of guilt, comparative classification in other CoE States

Criterion 3 — Severity of potential penalty: what matters is the penalty risked (maximum possible), not penalty actually imposed; deprivation of liberty as punishment is criminal except where not appreciably detrimental (Engel §85)
Criteria 2 and 3 are alternative: suffices that offence by nature is criminal OR penalty belongs to criminal sphere (Lutz v. Germany §55)
Cumulative approach may be adopted where separate analysis does not yield clear conclusion (Bendenoun §47)
alternatives: Hard core of criminal law: full Article 6 criminal guarantees with full stringency
Regulatory/administrative criminal charges (tax surcharges etc.): criminal-head guarantees apply but not necessarily with full stringency (Jussila §43)
defeaters: Hard core vs. regulatory distinction (Jussila): guarantees apply with reduced stringency outside traditional criminal law
Rules directed solely at specific professional groups may weigh against criminal classification
Disciplinary proceedings against judges classified under civil limb even where punitive (Ramos Nunes de Carvalho e Sá [GC], 2018)
DOCTRINE_ENGEL_CRITERIA.key_phrases: "criminal charge", "autonomous meaning", "Engel criteria", "nature of the offence", "severity of the penalty", "domestic classification", "generally binding character", "punitive and deterrent", "hard core of criminal law", "cumulative approach", "alternative and not cumulative", "substantive reality of the procedure"

DOCTRINE_SALDUZ_IBRAHIM.label: Salduz Doctrine / Ibrahim Two-Stage Test (Right to Lawyer)
DOCTRINE_SALDUZ_IBRAHIM.parent: Articles 6(1) and 6(3)(c) — right of access to lawyer
DOCTRINE_SALDUZ_IBRAHIM.establishing_cases: Salduz v. Turkey [GC] (2008), Ibrahim v. UK [GC] (2016), Beuze v. Belgium [GC] (2018), Dvorski v. Croatia [GC] (2015)
requires: [AND — Ibrahim two-stage test]
As a rule: access to a lawyer from the first interrogation of a suspect by police (Salduz §55)
Stage 1 — Were there compelling reasons for restriction? Must be: (i) exceptional circumstances, (ii) temporary, (iii) individual assessment, (iv) basis in domestic law (Ibrahim §258)
Stage 2 — Overall fairness assessment using Ibrahim §274 non-exhaustive factors
Content of right: (1) prior confidential consultation before interview; (2) physical presence during questioning (Beuze)
Right to lawyer of own choosing (Dvorski [GC])
alternatives: Legal framework and domestic exclusionary rules
Ability to challenge evidence at trial
Use of evidence and weight in conviction
defeaters: Valid waiver: knowing and intelligent, unequivocal, with minimum safeguards
Compelling reasons existed for temporary restriction (Ibrahim Stage 1)
Overall fairness maintained even without compelling reasons under very strict scrutiny (Ibrahim §§264-265; Beuze evolution)
General statutory restriction cannot amount to compelling reasons — individual case-specific assessment always required (Beuze §§142-144)
Absence of compelling reasons does not lead in itself to violation (Ibrahim §262) but weighs heavily in the balance
DOCTRINE_SALDUZ_IBRAHIM.typical_edge_pattern: (Police_Interrogation_Without_Lawyer) --[Ibrahim Stage 1]--> (Compelling_Reasons?) --[Stage 2]--> (Overall_Fairness_Assessment) --[very strict scrutiny if no compelling reasons]--> (Violation_or_Not)
DOCTRINE_SALDUZ_IBRAHIM.key_phrases: "access to a lawyer", "first interrogation", "first police interrogation", "compelling reasons", "overall fairness", "irretrievably prejudiced", "rights of the defence", "practical and effective", "very strict scrutiny", "knowing and intelligent waiver", "incriminating statements", "counterweight to vulnerability"

DOCTRINE_BANKOVIC_JURISDICTION.label: Banković Jurisdictional Test (Extraterritorial Jurisdiction Evolution)
DOCTRINE_BANKOVIC_JURISDICTION.parent: Article 1 jurisdiction — doctrinal development from restrictive to expansive
DOCTRINE_BANKOVIC_JURISDICTION.establishing_cases: Banković v. Belgium [GC] (2001, dec.), Al-Skeini v. UK [GC] (2011), Georgia v. Russia (II) [GC] (2021), Hanan v. Germany [GC] (2021), Ukraine and the Netherlands v. Russia (dec.) [GC] (2022)
requires: [AND — evolving framework]
Banković (2001): most restrictive — jurisdiction essentially territorial, no cause-and-effect, espace juridique limits Convention to legal space of Contracting States, no divided and tailored rights
Al-Skeini (2011): expansion — validated effective control + state agent authority simultaneously, permitted divided and tailored rights (§137), directly contradicting Banković

Georgia v. Russia (II) (2021): partial Banković resurrection for active hostilities — context of chaos excludes state agent authority; but detention during active phase + post-ceasefire occupation restore jurisdiction
Ukraine and Netherlands v. Russia (2022): explicitly abandoned Banković's divided and tailored prohibition (§571)
Hanan (2021): jurisdiction over German-ordered airstrike in Afghanistan via spatial control of military camp + exercise of public powers
defeaters: Active hostilities phase: context of chaos excludes both models (Georgia v. Russia (II))
Espace juridique: substantially diminished but not fully abandoned
Attribution to international organization (Behrami)
No pure cause-and-effect jurisdiction (Banković, still valid on this point)
DOCTRINE_BANKOVIC_JURISDICTION.key_phrases: "within their jurisdiction", "effective control", "state agent authority", "espace juridique", "primarily territorial", "exceptional circumstances", "public powers", "divided and tailored", "context of chaos", "active phase of hostilities"

DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION.label: N. v. UK to Paposhvili — Medical Expulsion Threshold
DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION.parent: Article 3 absolute prohibition — expulsion of seriously ill persons
DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION.establishing_cases: D. v. UK (1997), N. v. UK [GC] (2008), Paposhvili v. Belgium [GC] (2016), Savran v. Denmark [GC] (2021)
requires: [AND — Paposhvili threshold (§183)]
Very exceptional circumstances (high threshold maintained)
Person need not be at imminent risk of dying (lowered from N. v. UK 'close to death')
Real risk of: (a) serious, rapid and irreversible decline in state of health resulting in intense suffering, OR (b) significant reduction in life expectancy
On account of absence of appropriate treatment in receiving country or lack of access to such treatment
Applies irrespective of the nature of illness — both physical and mental (Savran [GC])
alternatives: Applicant must adduce evidence of substantial grounds for believing removal creates real risk
Burden then shifts to State to dispel doubts
State must verify care is sufficient and appropriate in practice in receiving country
State must consider whether individual will actually have access to care (cost, distance, social/family network)
If serious doubts persist: State must obtain individual and sufficient assurances from receiving State
defeaters: Threshold remains high — mere deterioration or reduction in quality of life insufficient
Diagnosis of serious mental illness alone not sufficient to engage Article 3 (Savran [GC])
Risk of relapse leading to aggressive behavior toward others insufficient — risk must be to applicant's own health (Savran)
Mental health cases may more readily engage Article 8 proportionality than Article 3 absolute threshold (Savran: violation of Art 8 but not Art 3)
State has verified actual access to adequate treatment in receiving country
DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION.typical_edge_pattern: (Medical_Expulsion) --[Paposhvili test]--> (Very_Exceptional_Circumstances?) --[serious rapid irreversible decline / reduced life expectancy?]--> (Procedural_Obligations_Met?) --[if not]--> (Violation)
DOCTRINE_PAPOSHVILI_MEDICAL_EXPULSION.key_phrases: "very exceptional circumstances", "close to death", "critically ill", "serious, rapid and irreversible decline", "intense suffering", "significant reduction in life expectancy", "absence of appropriate treatment", "lack of access to such treatment", "substantial grounds for believing", "dispel any doubts", "individual and sufficient assurances", "high threshold", "compelling humanitarian grounds", "seriously ill person"

DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING.label: Von Hannover / Bédat — Six-Criteria Privacy vs. Expression Balancing
DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING.parent: Article 8 vs. Article 10 — equal value, equal respect
DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING.establishing_cases: Von Hannover v. Germany (No. 1) (2004), Von Hannover v. Germany (No. 2) [GC] (2012), Axel Springer AG v. Germany [GC] (2012), Bédat v. Switzerland [GC] (2016), Satakunnan Markkinapörssi Oy v. Finland [GC] (2017)
requires: [AND — six criteria for balancing (Von Hannover (No. 2) + Axel Springer)]
Criterion 1 — Contribution to a debate of general interest: initial and essential criterion; extends beyond politics; distinguishes public debate from satisfying curiosity of a particular readership
Criterion 2 — How well known is the person / subject: public figures vs. private individuals; scrutiny of official functions more permissible than intrusion into private activities
Criterion 3 — Prior conduct of the person concerned: whether person courted media attention or cooperated with press, reducing legitimate expectation of privacy

Criterion 4 — Content, form and consequences of publication: text vs photos; informational vs sensationalist; reach; consequences for the person; photographs convey very personal or intimate information
Criterion 5 — Circumstances in which information/photographs were obtained: surreptitiously, with consent, through breach of confidence, or harassment
Criterion 6 — Severity of the sanction imposed (Axel Springer addition): including potential chilling effect on freedom of expression
alternatives: [OR — additional factor for criminal proceedings context]
Influence on the criminal proceedings: protection of presumption of innocence and fair trial rights (Bédat [GC])
defeaters: Where domestic courts applied the Court's criteria: strong reasons required to substitute Court's own view (procedural margin of appreciation)
Both rights of equal value and equal respect — outcome should not depend on which Article is invoked (Von Hannover (No. 2) §106)
Same criteria apply from both Article 8 and Article 10 perspectives
DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING.typical_edge_pattern: (Privacy_vs_Expression_Conflict) --[six criteria]--> (General_Interest + Public_Figure + Prior_Conduct + Content_Form + Circumstances + Sanction) --[balanced]--> (Art8_Prevails_or_Art10_Prevails)
DOCTRINE_VON_HANNOVER_BEDAT_BALANCING.key_phrases: "debate of general interest", "contribution to a debate of public interest", "public figure", "prior conduct", "content, form and consequences", "circumstances in which the photographs were taken", "severity of the sanction", "equal respect", "equal value", "strong reasons", "chilling effect", "responsible journalism", "legitimate expectation of privacy", "balancing exercise", "competing interests"

PROCEDURE_FRIENDLY_SETTLEMENT.label: Friendly Settlement (Article 39)
PROCEDURE_FRIENDLY_SETTLEMENT.parent: Article 39 ECHR; Rule 62 Rules of Court
PROCEDURE_FRIENDLY_SETTLEMENT.establishing_cases: Article 39 ECHR text; Rule 62; compulsory 12-week non-contentious phase since 2019
requires: [AND — conditions for friendly settlement]
Court places itself at disposal of parties (Rule 62 §1, typically at communication stage)
Settlement must be on the basis of respect for human rights as defined in the Convention
Negotiations strictly confidential (Article 39 §2) — cannot be referred to in subsequent proceedings
Both parties confirm agreed terms
Court publishes decision/judgment recording facts and terms, then strikes case out
alternatives: Registry may propose concrete terms in cases with established case law
Compulsory 12-week non-contentious phase precedes contentious proceedings (since 2019)
Committee of Ministers supervises execution of settlement terms
defeaters: Respect for human rights requires continued examination (human rights proviso)
Non-compliance with terms: Court may restore case to its list (Article 37 §2)
Settlement not on basis of respect for human rights
PROCEDURE_FRIENDLY_SETTLEMENT.key_phrases: "friendly settlement", "Article 39", "reached a settlement", "reached a friendly settlement", "terms of the settlement", "ex gratia", "strike the case out of its list", "brief statement of the facts and of the solution reached", "basis of respect for human rights", "Committee of Ministers", "non-contentious phase", "(friendly settlement)"

PROCEDURE_STRIKING_OUT.label: Striking Out (Article 37)
PROCEDURE_STRIKING_OUT.establishing_cases: Akman v. Turkey (2001), Tahsin Acar v. Turkey [GC] (2003), De Tommaso v. Italy [GC] (2017)
requires: [OR — three grounds under Article 37 §1]
Ground (a): applicant does not intend to pursue application (fails to respond, expressly withdraws, fails to pursue)
Ground (b): matter has been resolved — direct cause remedied and adequate redress provided domestically
Ground (c): catch-all — no longer justified to continue examination; most commonly for unilateral declarations
Human rights proviso: Court SHALL continue if respect for human rights so requires, even when striking-out criteria are met
alternatives: Government acknowledges violation and offers redress without applicant's consent
Assessed under Tahsin Acar [GC] principles: nature of complaints, adequacy of redress, whether well-established case law exists
Court may reject if case raises novel issue (De Tommaso [GC]) or redress is inadequate
If accepted: case struck out with Committee of Ministers supervision of execution
defeaters: Human rights proviso: novel issues, serious abuses, insufficient case law, inadequate remedial measures require continued examination
Unilateral declaration inadequate: novel issue, insufficient redress, no well-established case law
Non-compliance with unilateral declaration terms permits restoration to list (Article 37 §2)
PROCEDURE_STRIKING_OUT.typical_edge_pattern: (Striking_Out_Request) --[which ground?]--> (Non_Pursuit / Resolved / Catch_All) --[human rights proviso]--> (Continue_or_Strike_Out) --[CM supervision if struck out]
PROCEDURE_STRIKING_OUT.key_phrases: "strike out", "struck out of the list", "struck out of its list of cases", "Article 37", "does not intend to pursue", "matter has been resolved", "no longer justified to continue the examination", "respect for human rights", "unilateral declaration", "restore to the list", "Tahsin Acar", "(striking out)"

PROCEDURE_THIRD_PARTY_INTERVENTION.label: Third-Party Intervention (Article 36)
PROCEDURE_THIRD_PARTY_INTERVENTION.parent: Article 36 ECHR; Rule 44 Rules of Court
PROCEDURE_THIRD_PARTY_INTERVENTION.establishing_cases: Article 36 ECHR text; Rule 44; Protocol No. 14 (adding §3, in force 2010)
requires: [OR — three pathways]
Article 36 §1 — National State of applicant (as of right): automatic right, no leave required; may submit written comments and participate in hearings (Rule 44 §1)

Article 36 §2 — By leave of the President: open to any other Contracting State or any person concerned (NGOs, academics, NHRIs, individuals); request must be duly reasoned, submitted within 12 weeks of notification; normally limited to written observations; oral participation only in exceptional circumstances
Article 36 §3 — Commissioner for Human Rights (ex officio): added by Protocol No. 14; no leave required; may intervene in any case before Chamber or Grand Chamber on own initiative
defeaters: §2 intervention: request not duly reasoned or outside 12-week deadline
§2 intervention: not in the interest of the proper administration of justice
Intervention limited to written observations unless exceptional circumstances
PROCEDURE_THIRD_PARTY_INTERVENTION.key_phrases: "third-party intervention", "third party intervention", "Article 36", "leave to intervene", "granted leave to intervene", "written observations", "written comments", "in the interest of the proper administration of justice", "any person concerned", "Commissioner for Human Rights", "amicus curiae", "Rule 44"

PROCEDURE_RULE_39_INTERIM_MEASURES.label: Rule 39 Interim Measures
PROCEDURE_RULE_39_INTERIM_MEASURES.parent: Rule 39 Rules of Court; binding under Article 34 (Mamatkulov [GC])
PROCEDURE_RULE_39_INTERIM_MEASURES.establishing_cases: Cruz Varas v. Sweden (1991, overruled), Mamatkulov and Askarov v. Turkey [GC] (2005), Paladi v. Moldova [GC] (2009)
requires: [AND — conditions for indication]
Exceptional circumstances only
Imminent risk of irreparable harm to a Convention right
Harm of nature that would not be susceptible to reparation, restoration or adequate compensation
Necessary in the interests of the parties or the proper conduct of the proceedings
Binding character: non-compliance constitutes violation of Article 34 right of individual application (Mamatkulov [GC] §128, overruling Cruz Varas)
alternatives: Expulsion/deportation cases: risk under Articles 2 or 3 (vast majority)
Extradition: death penalty or torture risk
defeaters: No imminent risk of irreparable harm
No exceptional circumstances
Non-compliance standard: Government must take all reasonable steps to comply (Paladi [GC]); intentions/reasons are irrelevant
Article 34 violation is separate finding regardless of whether substantive violation is found on merits (Mamatkulov itself: no Art 2/3/6 violation but Art 34 violated)
PROCEDURE_RULE_39_INTERIM_MEASURES.key_phrases: "interim measure", "interim measures", "Rule 39", "Rule 39 of the Rules of Court", "indicated to the Government", "not to expel", "not to extradite", "not to deport", "not to remove", "binding character", "binding nature", "imminent risk of irreparable harm", "irreversible situations", "Article 34", "right of individual application", "failure to comply", "all reasonable steps"

PROCEDURE_SEPARATE_OPINIONS.label: Separate and Dissenting Opinions
PROCEDURE_SEPARATE_OPINIONS.parent: Article 45 §2 ECHR; Rule 74 §2 Rules of Court
PROCEDURE_SEPARATE_OPINIONS.establishing_cases: Article 45 §2 ECHR text; Rule 74; Notable doctrinal impact: dissents in Rees (1986) and Cossey (1990) became majority in Christine Goodwin [GC] (2002)
requires: [OR — types of separate opinion]
Concurring opinion: agrees with outcome but different/additional reasoning
Partly dissenting opinion: agrees on some findings, disagrees on others
Dissenting opinion: disagrees with judgment's conclusion entirely
Partly concurring, partly dissenting opinion: mixed agreement and disagreement
Joint opinion: co-authored by multiple judges (Rule 74 §4)
Bare statement of dissent: simple disagreement without reasoning (rarely used)
alternatives: Operative part states vote count: 'Holds, by X votes to Y...'
Then: 'In accordance with Article 45 § 2 of the Convention and Rule 74 § 2...'
Each opinion appears after main judgment with CAPS heading
defeaters: Unanimous judgment: no separate opinions needed (though concurrences still possible)
Only 6% of judgments of little legal significance attract separate opinions vs. 56% of significant judgments
PROCEDURE_SEPARATE_OPINIONS.key_phrases: "I voted against", "I cannot agree", "I cannot agree with the majority", "I regret that I am unable to follow the majority", "To my regret, I am unable to share the view", "With great respect, I disagree", "I respectfully dissent", "In my view", "I find myself unable to subscribe to", "I reach a different conclusion", "In accordance with Article 45 § 2"

case_law_hierarchy.description: No formal stare decisis. Grand Chamber judgments carry highest authority; Committee judgments should not be cited as authority on points of principle.

grand_chamber.authority: Highest — functions as binding on Chambers

committee.authority: Applies well-established case-law only; should not be cited as authority on points of principle

relinquishment_art30.rule_72_para1: MAY relinquish for serious interpretive question
relinquishment_art30.rule_72_para2: SHALL relinquish where resolution might be inconsistent with prior case-law

referral_art43.criteria: Serious question of interpretation/application or serious issue of general importance
referral_art43.decision: Panel of five GC judges (no reasons for refusal)

departing_from_precedent.approach: Court 'clarifies' or 'develops' — rarely says 'overrule'. GC is primary vehicle.
notable: Christine Goodwin v. UK [GC] (2002) — reversing Sheffield and Horsham on transgender rights
Bayatyan v. Armenia [GC] (2011) — departing on conscientious objection
Lautsi v. Italy [GC] (2011) — GC reversing Chamber on crucifixes
Al-Skeini v. UK [GC] (2011) — departing from Banković on extraterritorial jurisdiction

hudoc_importance.key_cases: Highest — significant contribution to development/clarification/modification of case-law
hudoc_importance.legacy_note: 'Case Reports' is historical terminology from older reporting practice

editorial_conventions.absolutist_language: must/never/always in 'requires'/'defeaters' fields = Court's own categorical holdings. Narrative uses softer language unless truly categorical.
editorial_conventions.primary_vs_secondary: All concept source_type is primary (judgments). Guides/factsheets are secondary (not binding).
